topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my phone screen, a bright, unwelcome intrusion into my otherwise quiet afternoon. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that seemed to steal all the oxygen from the room. I fumbled for the device, my fingers clumsy and slick with sudden cold sweat. It was a post from Mr. Harrison, my history teacher. A picture of him smiling, taken at what looked like a casual gathering, and the caption was a cryptic, vaguely academic quote. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat against my sternum. I quickly minimized the app, my gaze darting around the classroom as if expecting him to materialize from behind the whiteboard. Every rustle of paper, every distant cough, sounded amplified, threatening. I felt a dizzying urge to bolt, to disappear, but my feet were rooted to the linoleum floor.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the image of Mr. Davies’ face staring back at me, somehow magnified by the sheer wrongness of the situation. My hands started trembling so violently I had to press them flat against my desk, trying to anchor myself to something solid. A strange, hot flush crept up my neck, making my skin feel tight and prickly. Why was he here? Why was this happening? My mind raced through every possible scenario, each one a cascade of dreadful possibilities. I snatched my phone, intending to delete the notification instantly, but my thumb hovered over the screen, paralyzed. I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving a clammy residue. The silence of the lecture hall suddenly felt suffocating, heavy with unspoken scrutiny.

[story 3]

It happened so fast. One second, I was scrolling through mundane updates, the next, a direct message popped up from Professor Evans. The text was short, just an emoji and a reference to a shared assignment. My stomach lurched, a sickening drop in my chest. I dropped my phone onto my lap, the sound surprisingly loud in the relative quiet of the library. I squeezed my eyes shut for a brief second, trying to force my racing thoughts into a manageable sequence. When I opened them, the world seemed slightly skewed. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a deep, uncomfortable warmth spreading through my torso. I needed to leave, needed distance, but my legs felt strangely heavy, as if moving required immense, impossible effort.
